coredata.dc:title: Poly-L-lysine functionalized graphene quantum dots embedded zirconium metal–organic framework-based fluorescence switch on-off-on nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detection of taurine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Nangare, Sopan
Chaudhari, Kalyani
Patil, Pravin
coredata.dc:description: Taurine is now widely used as a novel diagnostic biomarker for cardiovascular disease. Unfortunately, engaged techniques for the analysis of taurine are suffering from low sensitivity, poor selectivity, and a tedious process. In this work, the poly-L-lysine functionalized graphene quantum dots embedded UiO-66@NH2 metal–organic framework (PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF) based turn ‘On-Off-On’ fluorescent nanoprobe was designed for taurine sensing. Initially, synthesized PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe were confirmed using UV–Vis spectroscopy, FT-IR analysis, particle size analysis, fluorescence study, PXRD, SEM-EDAX, HR-TEM, etc. Here, anticipated PLL-fGQDs incorporation into UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe portrayed a highly fluorescent nanoprobe for taurine sensing. The copper (Cu2+) ions addition in the fluorescent nanoprobe resulted in fluorescence dampness followed by taurine addition showed the recovery of quenched fluorescence. Principally, it might be because of the greater binding affinity of Cu2+ ions towards taurine-containing oxygen atoms of a sulfonic group and the nitrogen atom of an amine group. The linear concentration range, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ) of taurine were found to be 5 ng/mL to 360 ng/mL, 2.91 ng/mL, and 8.84 ng/mL, respectively. It also provides a high selectivity towards taurine in the occurrence of interfering agents whereas practical applicability was assured by spiked artificial serum samples analysis. In conclusion, the designed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe offers high sensitivity, selectivity, good stability, repeatability, and practicability. In the future, it can be used as a new nanoprobe for taurine recognition with improved performance than the traditional methods.

head.ce:title: Poly-L-lysine functionalized graphene quantum dots embedded zirconium metal–organic framework-based fluorescence switch on-off-on nanoprobe for highly sensitive and selective detection of taurine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Taurine is now widely used as a novel diagnostic biomarker for cardiovascular disease. Unfortunately, engaged techniques for the analysis of taurine are suffering from low sensitivity, poor selectivity, and a tedious process. In this work, the poly-L-lysine functionalized graphene quantum dots embedded UiO-66@NH2 metal–organic framework (PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF) based turn ‘On-Off-On’ fluorescent nanoprobe was designed for taurine sensing. Initially, synthesized PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe were confirmed using UV–Vis spectroscopy, FT-IR analysis, particle size analysis, fluorescence study, PXRD, SEM-EDAX, HR-TEM, etc. Here, anticipated PLL-fGQDs incorporation into UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe portrayed a highly fluorescent nanoprobe for taurine sensing. The copper (Cu2+) ions addition in the fluorescent nanoprobe resulted in fluorescence dampness followed by taurine addition showed the recovery of quenched fluorescence. Principally, it might be because of the greater binding affinity of Cu2+ ions towards taurine-containing oxygen atoms of a sulfonic group and the nitrogen atom of an amine group. The linear concentration range, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ) of taurine were found to be 5 ng/mL to 360 ng/mL, 2.91 ng/mL, and 8.84 ng/mL, respectively. It also provides a high selectivity towards taurine in the occurrence of interfering agents whereas practical applicability was assured by spiked artificial serum samples analysis. In conclusion, the designed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe offers high sensitivity, selectivity, good stability, repeatability, and practicability. In the future, it can be used as a new nanoprobe for taurine recognition with improved performance than the traditional methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Presently, cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) are a leading consequence of mortality. Traditional approaches are unable to predict this increased risk [1]. In this shade, biomarker identification is pivotal in healthcare for diagnosis. Therefore, research scholars are now investigating for novel biomarkers for the diagnosis of plenty of serious disorders such as CVD [2]. Importantly, CVD can be managed satisfactorily at a preliminary phase by monitoring divergent biochemical values. In a sense, it is imperative to emphasize that CVD can be spotted at an initial stage by adopting an innovative biosensing strategy [3]. Taurine (2-aminoethanesulfonic acid) [4] is a free and necessary amino acid. It is present in a variety of body fluids, including plasma and urine. As well, it is committed to a variety of crucial biological processes [5]. As a result, taurine is a significant biomarker for the earliest identification of CVD [6]. In the literature, multiple methods have been developed for the determination of taurine [7,8]. Nonetheless, there are several shortcomings of these methods such as the difficult extraction, need for modern equipment, high pricing, prolonged detection process, etc., [9] that need to be overcome using advanced methods.

Carbon-based zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials namely graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are ordinarily prioritized for biomedical applications encompassing such diagnostics and others [10]. Mainly, GQDs are tiny graphene particles with diameters less than 100 nm [11]. It offers superior and dynamic physicochemical features which are important for the development of the sensing approach [12]. The surface modulation of GQDs employing functionalized molecules, and other doping agents has been extensively outlined [13] for several biomedical applications. In brief, surface functionalization provides a profusion of GQDs containing exterior organic groups with intense fluorescence behavior, rendering them attractive choices for specific taurine monitoring [14]. Interestingly, both GQDs and functionalized GQDs have pH-dependent photoluminescence properties, and functional group protonation/deprotonation enables a potential tactic for modulating the bandgap of GQDs by regulating the charge distribution across GQDs and functional groups [15]. It has been mentioned that surface passivation with functionalizing agents including poly-L-lysine (PLL) and polyethyleneimine can strengthen quantum yield (QY) because of reducing traps and flaws [16]. The enlarged sp2 carbon domains following amination are attributable to the enhanced photoluminescence intensity. As well, amino groups have a greater aptitude to contribute electrons over hydroxyl groups, thereby boosting emission [17]. Here, it showed high responsiveness and boosted specificity. As a corollary, we intend to adopt PLL for GQD functionalization in favor of boosting the output of fluorescent nanoprobe. Despite surface modification of GQDs, there is a risk of agglomeration or precipitation during analyte concentration measurement. As a result, the practical sensing applications of probes may be limited. Herein, the consistent decorating of PLL-fGQDs into porous frameworks may aid in their agglomeration prevention [18].

Currently, the research fraternity has broadly reported the preference for advanced functionalized luminous nanomaterials for several applications [19,20] that include biomedical applications [21]. Metal-organic framework (MOF) is perforated and crystalline coordination polymers. Principally, it is mostly constituted of metal ions or clusters and chelating ligands [22]. For sensing purposes, researchers are particularly intrigued by zirconium and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA) facilitated UiO-66@NH2 MOF (UiO-66: Universitetet I Oslo). The literature survey divulged the intriguing physicochemical characteristics of UiO-66@NH2 MOF [23] which are beneficial for sensing as well as other biomedical and environmental applications. As an output, UiO-66@NH2 MOF-based sensor revealed adequate sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and real-time applicability for the recognition of an analyte [24]. Despite these game-changing advantages, MOF has severe drawbacks, particularly the breakdown of MOF structures and the shrinking of pores [25]. As a result, there is a requirement for additional nanomaterials that can help to overcome both inadequacies while keeping the original properties of MOF. Therefore, we intend to incorporate PLL-fGQDs into the UiO-66@NH2 MOF to tackle these limitations.

Fascinatingly, the highly porous nature of MOF may offer abundant binding sites for the functionalized GQDs. As a result, it will furnish the fluorescent nanoprobe with combined charming characteristics of GQDs, and MOF [18]. Recently, the encapsulation of GQDs into MOF has been reported to offer better photoluminescence characteristics. Moreover, the employment of inorganic quantum dots (QDs) with MOF has been accounted to improve the oxidase activity. Interestingly, the quercetin recognition has been documented using carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated ZIF-8 wherein, it showed a lower limit of detection (LOD) and high selectivity [26]. As well, the use of MOF in combination with CDs and QDs offered strong adsorption followed by high sensitivity and selectivity for the revealing of ascorbic acid [18]. The combination of CDs with copper nanoclusters and their encapsulation into ZIF showed the potential to identify uric acid in human urine samples with high sensitivity and selectivity [27]. Moreover, it has been suggested that the surface-modified fluorescent nanomaterial encapsulation in MOF can make a superior sensory composite than their bare one [26]. As a result, we consider that incorporating PLL-fGQDs into UiO-66@NH2 MOF will integrate the benefits of all of these component groups to create a sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe. To the best of our current understanding, no study on the conjugation of PLL-fGQDs with UiO-66@NH2 MOF for sensory applications has been documented.

In this research, we designed the fluorescence-based PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe for taurine towering monitoring. In brief, the use of PLL as a functionalizing agent for GQDs helps to tune the quantum yield (QY) and optical properties of the bare GQDs obtained from a green precursor. Importantly, the integrated form of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe produced a brighter fluorescence than the separate PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Moreover, the PLL-fGQDs incorporation offered the supporting agent for UiO-66@NH2 MOF that can benefit from avoiding structural collapse. After the addition of copper (Cu2+) ions, the fluorescence of anticipated PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was found to be quenched whereas the addition of taurine into the quenched system resulted in the recovery of fluorescence. As an output of this research work, the design of the nanoprobe offered high responsiveness and specificity for taurine. Here, the coordination between the taurine-containing oxygen atom of a sulfonic group and the nitrogen atom of the amine group with Cu2+ ions could be the reason for the recovery of quenched fluorescence of the nanoprobe. Finally, it offered a superior presentation to previously reported methods in terms of the linearity range, limit of quantification (LOQ), LOD, etc. Moreover, PLL-GQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe showed high specificity for taurine in the presence of other interfering substances. In the future, the design of the PLL-GQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe will offer a one-of-a-kind, straightforward, effective, extremely responsive, and specific fluorescent nanoprobe for taurine determination.

Black-eyed bean shells were collected from the Shirpur local market (Dhule), India. Sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH, ≥ 97.0 %) were obtained from Merck Specialties Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. Poly-L-lysine (PLL, Mol. Wt. > 50,000), and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (2-ATA, 99 %), were procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4, 98 %) was obtained from Chemica-Biochemica reagents, India. Taurine (Mol. Wt. 125.1, 99 %) was obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. Taloja − 410 208, Maharashtra, India. Dimethyl-formamide (DMF), phosphate buffer tablets (pH 7.4), and quinine sulfate were collected from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade deionized water (DI, 0.2 µm filtered) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Avantor Performance Materials India Ltd. Thane, India.

In brief, the GQDs were made from black-eyed beans shell powder using teflon-lined stainless steel (SS) hydrothermal. Firstly, 2 g of black-eyed bean shell powder was added to 40 mL of DI water and then mixed using a magnetic stirrer at 200 rpm for 2 h. Here, the produced 40 mL dispersion was placed in a teflon-lined autoclave in a SS hydrothermal reactor (12 h at 160 °C). Following this hydrothermal method, the greenish solution turned into a dark-brown solution that demonstrated the GQDs synthesis. The resultant solution pH was obtained to be 7. After the hydrothermal procedure, the obtained GQDs solution was subjected to bath sonication for 25 min at 30 °C to give a homogeneous dispersion of GQDs. In addition, the purification of GQDs was accomplished using dialysis for 24 h adopting a dialysis bag (0.22 μm, 12,000 kDa). Finally, the completed dialyzed GQDs solution was centrifuged (Refrigerated Centrifuge, Elteck Overseas Pvt. India) at 20000 rpm (20 min, 10 °C to 15 °C) to eliminate visible contaminants. Here, freeze-drying was preferred for the drying of obtained GQDs. In this case, pre-freezing (Southern Scientific Lab Instrument, India) was accomplished at −40 °C for 12 h wherein GQDs solution was converted into solid form. Then, primary drying was performed using a laboratory freeze drier (Southern Scientific Lab Instrument, India) at −40 °C and 0.016 mbar for 18 h. After that, the secondary drying was accomplished at 10 °C for 8 h and 25 °C for the next 4 h. At last, the obtained freeze-dried GQDs were subjected to −20 °C in sealed glass vials for further use.

In this study, 20 mg of GQDs were added to 100 mL of DI water to produce the GQDs stock. After that, the pH of the various GQDs was adjusted to be between 3 and 13 in a clean volumetric flask. Here, a freshly made 1.0 M solution of NaOH and 1.0 M HCl was used to change the pH levels of the GQDs solution at a temperature of 25 °C. The pH of the GQDs solution was then checked using a digital pH meter. To complete the reaction, prepared GQDs samples were given 60 min after the pH had been confirmed [28]. The UV cabinet and spectrofluorometer were utilized to determine the effect of pH on GQD fluorescence behavior. Finally, the effect of pH on GQD zeta potential was studied.

For functionalization of GQDs, PLL (300 μL) was blended with 10 mL GQDs (100 μg/mL) and then processed at 40 °C for 3 h using constant stirring at 300 rpm. Herein, the obtained PLL and GQDs combination was maintained untouched for 30 min at 25 °C, which helped to complete the functionalization of the GQDs surface. Then, the dark-brown color fluid was centrifuged for 10 min at 25,000 rpm before being incorporated into 10 mL of DI water. At last, the PLL-fGQDs solution was then lyophilized via a laboratory freeze drier using the abovementioned procedure (Scheme S1).

In this work, UiO-66@NH2 MOF was synthesized employing the formerly documented process with a small modification [24]. First, 374.7 mg of ZrCl4 was dissolved in 70 mL of DMF in a volumetric flask with a capacity of 100 mL. Then, 297.2 mg of 2-ATA was added and transformed to sonication for 1 h at 30 °C. After sonication, the obtained solution was loaded in a teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 120 °C for 26 h. After this step, the resultant dispersion was centrifuged for 15 min at 12000 rpm at 27° C, which helped to separate the UiO-66@NH2 MOF from the solvent. After that, the solution was rinsed three times with ethanol and then washed using DI water (twice). The final UiO-66@NH2 MOF (pH 7) was freeze-dried using the abovementioned drying procedure whereas various spectroscopic methods were used to examine the freeze-dried UiO-66@NH2 MOF.

In this study, PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was manufactured in a teflon-lined autoclave using PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF. In summary, UiO-66@NH2 MOF was synthesized by adopting the stated approach with a small modification. To begin, 20 mg of PLL-fGQDs was thoroughly dissolved in 20 mL of DMF (1000 µg/mL). After that, the aforementioned dispersion was processed with 374.7 mg of ZrCl4 dissolved in 50 mL of DMF and 297.2 mg of 2-ATA before being sonicated for 25 min at 30 °C. The aforementioned dispersion was then coupled with the PLL-fGQDs solution and then subjected to sonication (1 h at 30 °C). The resulting mixture of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was poured into a teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 120 °C for 26 h. At last, the obtained PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe dispersion was centrifuged for 15 min at 12000 rpm (temperature: 27 °C). The final PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was rinsed three times with ethanol and then with DI water to remove the undesirable material. Then, the found PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was processed for lyophilization at the abovementioned procedure (section under 2.2.1.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of GQDs, PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet (UV)-visible (Vis) spectrum of synthesized GQDs, PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe (50 μg/mL) were examined via a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan, quartz cuvette-path length: 1 cm, scanning range of 800 nm-200 nm) using DI water as a blank (reference). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was completed using an FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1S Shimadzu) [29]. Afterward, 50 μg/mL of prepared nanomaterials were used for fluorescence investigations via a UV cabinet (Southern Scientific Lab Instrument, in Chennai, India) at preselected wavelengths, including 254 nm shorter, 365 nm longer wavelengths, and visible light. After this confirmation, pH determination of GQDs and PLL-fGQDs was performed by a digital pH meter (Phoenix system 362, systronics, India). Diffractograms were used to confirm the crystallinity of obtained nanomaterials. In summary, 10 mg of each sample was examined through an X-ray diffractometer (Philips analytical XRD, PW 3710) at 40 kV at a regulated temperature using a copper tube with a nickel Kα filter. The fluorescence testing of each sample with a specific concentration (50 ng/mL) was accomplished via a spectrofluorometer (JASCO International CO. Ltd. Japan) at 25 °C. Concisely, the excitation and emission spectrums were optimized by altering the excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 420 nm. The fluorescence intensity of Uio-66@NH2 MOF and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was measured at optimized excitation wavelengths between 200 nm and 700 nm. The Nanoplus 3 particulate system (Micromeritics, USA) was used to assess the zeta potentials of nanomaterials. The surface morphologies and the mass percentage of multi-elements of synthesized nanomaterials were explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), set with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX, 7610F Plus/JEOL, Hisar, Haryana) at a voltage of 20 kV and a high vacuum. The Raman analysis was completed using Horiba JY with excitation lasers (633 nm). The chosen excitation source was first focused with a 100X objective lens as well as laser power (1 mW). The Raman signal for a preferred sample was captured using a silicon-based multi-channel array detector (CCD) in a backscattering arrangement. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) total pore volumes and pore size of freeze-dried UiO-66NH2 MOF were measured from nitrogen (N2) adsorption isotherms using surface area and pore analyzers (Nova 600 BET). Then, HR-TEM (HR-TEM, Jeol/JEM 2100) with a LaB6 light source at 200 kV was used to examine the shape, and size of PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe.At last, the average particle size of PLL-fGQDs was calculated using ImageJ software (N = 20).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence percent QY and biocompatibility analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The 'QY' of greenly generated GQDs and PLL-fGQDs was estimated using Equation S1[30]. (Brief details provided in supplementary data ). In addition, we have assessed the in vitro cytotoxicity of the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe using the MMT [MTT: 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide] assay on the FHC CRL-1831 cell line, a normal large intestine colon cell line. FHC CRL-1831 cells were cultivated in 96-well plates at a density of 1*104 cells and exposed to various concentrations of the prepared PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. The culture medium was replaced with serum-free Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM), and MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was introduced to each well. Following a 24 h incubation at 37 °C in darkness, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution was applied to dissolve MTT formazan crystals after removing the MTT solution. Lastly, absorbance at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader [31].

In this step, fresh fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe (100 μg/mL) solution was prepared using pH 7.4 PBS for further analysis of taurine. Initially, the fluorescence of the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was confirmed at optimized parameters. After this, the different concentrations of Cu2+ ions ranging from 1 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL were prepared in pH 7.4 PBS and added into the fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobes, accordingly. The quenching efficiency of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was confirmed with respective Cu2+ ions concentrations. After optimization of Cu2+ ions concentrations, the detection of taurine was accomplished. In this case, different concentrations of taurine ranging from 5 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL were prepared using pH 7.4 PBS. After optimization, the quenched Cu2+-PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobes were prepared and then preferred for sample analysis. Here, each prepared taurine sample was incorporated into the separate quenched PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. After next, the nanoprobe was reserved aside for 10 min. Then, the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe fluorescence intensity was considered for all taurine samples via a spectrofluorometer. In the final, the recovery of fluorescence intensity of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was established with respective taurine concentrations. The LOQ [32] and the LOD of taurine were calculated using slope (m) and standard deviations (ϭ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference potential and other analytical parameter analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Several interfering agents were preferred along with taurine to validate the anti-interference potential of the anticipated fluorescent nanoprobe. In brief, solutions (100 ng/mL) of chosen interfering agents such as potassium chloride, sodium chloride, glucose, cysteine, lysine, glycine, alanine, albumin, taurine, etc. were prepared separately in pH 7.4 (PBS). Firstly, the above-prepared quenched PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was prepared. After this, 5 mL of quenched Cu2+-PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was mixed with 1 mL of each interference substance in a 10 mL volumetric flask. At this point, the nanoprobe was located away for 30 min at 25 °C to complete the interaction between nanoprobes and an interfering material. The PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe fluorescence intensity was then determined, and the same experiments were conducted with all interfering compounds and taurine. In addition, the collective outcome of all interfering components along with taurine (Sample Q) on the fluorescence potential of the fashioned nanoprobe was evaluated. A spiked sample was used to do a real-time measurement of taurine to confirm the application applicability of the proposed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. In brief, the artificial serum was mixed with 40 ng/mL and 60 ng/mL quantities of taurine. Herein, taurine spiked in artificial serum was recognized via sensing the aforementioned approach. At last, the taurine recovery percentage was determined using slope (m) and intercept. After this confirmation, to ensure the repeatability of the anticipated PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe for taurine detection, different nine nanoprobes were prepared. Here, the fluorescence intensity of each freshly prepared nanoprobe was measured, and then 400 ng/mL of taurine was added to the nanoprobe, separately. As an output, the recovered fluorescence intensity of the anticipated PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was measured. Finally, the percent relative standard deviation (% RSD) was calculated to assure the repeatability of the proposed fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe for taurine recognition.

The UV–Vis band of the green synthesized GQDs exhibited a strong absorption peak at a wavelength of 264 nm, which is ascribed to the C = C bond containing the π-π* transition. Here, it assured the aromatic carbon core existence in GQDs. Besides, the shoulder peak was obtained at 326 nm attributed to the C = O bond containing n-π* transition [33]. In conclusion, it confirms the flourishing green synthesis of GQDs from natural precursors. Secondly, the UV–Vis spectra of PLL-fGQDs disclosed a broad absorption peak at 266 nm, and 328 nm, which might be due to the masking of GQD surface functioning by PLL-containing amine (NH2) sites. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of UiO-66@NH2 MOF displayed two absorption peaks first at 220 nm, 256 nm, and broadband around 330 nm. In this, the maxima at 220 nm are credited to ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) [34]. The interactions between amino groups with a lone pair of electrons and the benzene ring with π* orbital are other factors that influence the second peak maxima. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe showed a wide absorption peak at the center of 283 nm and a weak absorption peak at 320 nm with absorption range of 200 nm to 600 nm, showing the combined absorption of PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Consequently, it guaranteed the absolute synthesis of PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF-mediated nanoprobe. The UV–Vis absorption of GQDs, PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe is revealed in Figure S1-
A. The obtained GQDs confirmed pale yellow color in visible light whereas it showed green fluorescence under λmax 254 nm light and bright blue fluorescence under UV light under λmax 365 nm. Possibly, it is because of electronic energy transition [35]. Overall, synthesized GQDs material shows fluorescence in longer UV wavelengths, which verifies the successful synthesis GQDs. In the shade of PLL-fGQDs, it also looks like a yellowish-brown color in visible light. It showed green and bright blue fluorescence under light with λmax 254 nm and λmax 365 nm, respectively [35]. The fluorescence potential of UiO-66@NH2 MOF was confirmed in the UV cabinet. As an effect, it furnishes creamish white in visible light with green fluorescence under λmax 254 nm light and bright blue fluorescence under longer wavelength λmax 365 nm [34]. The administration of an optimized concentration of constructed luminous UiO-66@NH2 MOF into a solution of PLL-fGQDs drives the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe to increase. Here, UiO-66@NH2 MOF-like creamish color was found in visible light. The green fluorescence was obtained under λmax 254 nm light and bright blue fluorescence under UV light λmax 365 nm light. Figure S1-
B depicts fluorescence analysis in the UV cabinet of obtained nanomaterials. Figure S2-
A depicts the fluorescence spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 360 nm to 420 nm. Herein, the shift in spectra was obtained towards the red shift whenever the wavelength changes. In this case, the highest intensity and Gaussian spectra were found at 395 nm. The excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of greenly produced GQD revealed significant excitation and emission peaks around 395 nm and 482 nm, respectively (
Figure S2-
B).

Fig. 1-A depicts the fluorescence intensity of pH-dependent changes in the prepared GQDs at different ranges from pH 3 to pH 13. In this study, fabricated GQDs solution exhibited a stable and bestowed maximum fluorescence at pH 9. While at alkaline pH (11 and 13), GQDs exhibited a decrease in fluorescence intensity whereas acidic pH (3 and 5) demonstrated less fluorescence. After that, fluorescence spectroscopy confirmed the pH influence on produced GQDs. Fig. 1 -B depicts the determination of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs at various pH. In acidic conditions, lower fluorescence intensity was found. Also, the pH varies from acidic to basic resulting in an augment in fluorescence intensity at pH 9 and then a reduction in fluorescence intensity from pH 11 to 13. Here, fluorescence spectra ensured the higher fluorescence intensity at pH 9. Possibly, it may be because of the protonation state of GQDs that affects the fluorescence intensity. To support this, the zeta potential of each sample was performed. Figure S3 depicts the zeta potential at various pH levels obtained to be at 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13. An acidic pH solution containing hydrogen ions (H+) was higher in the samples, and the zeta potential value resulted in a positive. As such, the hydroxyl ions (OH–) were increasing in number so the negative charge on the molecule increased. Accordingly, the zeta potential value was obtained to be negative. Overall, changes in zeta potential and fluorescence intensity might be because of the protonation or de-protonation concept [36].

The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs displayed the peak at 3301 cm−1, 1626 cm−1, 1533 cm−1, and 1070 cm−1 confirming the presence of stretching vibrations of different carboxylic functionality that includes the –OH, C = C, C = O, and C-O-C, respectively (Fig. 2
-A). Fig. 2 -B depicts the FT-IR spectrum of PLL-fGQDs. In this, peaks at 3344 cm−1, 1652 cm−1, and 1030 cm−1 assured the existence of vibrations of different functional groups such as N-H/O-H, C = O, and C-O, respectively. Additionally, a peak at 1352 cm−1 assured the bonding between aromatic carbon and nitrogen. Hence, it confirmed the synthesis of PLL-fGQDs. Fig. 2 -C depicts the FT-IR spectrum of prepared UiO-66@NH2 MOF. In this, peaks at 3311 cm−1, 2963 cm−1-2902 cm−1, 1641 cm−1, 1391 cm−1, 1067 cm−1, and 653 cm−1 confirmed the stretching vibrations of –OH, C-H, C = O, C-N, and C-O, accordingly. As well, the peak at 653 cm−1 guaranteed the occurrence of Zr-O bond vibrations in fabricated MOF. Overall, it assured the synthesis of the UiO-66@NH2 MOF. The FT-IR spectra of conjugated PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe (Fig. 2 -D) demonstrated the absorption peak at 3306 cm−1, due to stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH. Herein, the peak intensity of C = O was found to decrease as compared to the UiO-66@NH2 MOF and a little bit increase as compared to PLL-fGQDs. Principally, it may be because of weak electrostatic interaction between PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Moreover, the masking of C-H stretching of PLL-fGQDs was obtained at 2963 cm−1 of UiO-66@NH2 MOF, which confirmed the conjugation.

The zeta potential of GQDs was obtained to be −30.87 mV, which assured the existence of oxygen-based functionalities such as carboxyl (-C = O), and hydroxyl (–OH) on the exterior (
Figure S4-
A). It validated the GQDs synthesis with acceptable aqueous stability. After surface functionalization, the zeta potential of PLL-fGQDs was observed to be + 25.55 mV, which confirmed more stability than the bare GQDs ( Figure S4-
B). Notably, changes in the potential of PLL-fGQDs were ascribed. It may be due to the presence of a PLL-containing amino group on a surface. These amine-modified UiO-66@NH2 MOF showed the + 23.69 mV of zeta potential confirming the water stability of the dispersion ( Figure S4-
C). Herein, because UiO-66@NH2 MOF encloses zirconium and amine groups which have a positive charge on their surface. Figure S4-
D depicts the final nanoprobe PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe zeta potential. In brief, a zeta potential of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was found to be + 30.50 mV indicating the absorption of PLL-fGQDs by UiO-66@NH2 MOF through van der Waal forces. Consequently, it confirmed the formation of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. In conclusion, zeta potential analysis assured the good stability of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobes in the aqueous phase. Diffractogram of GQDs exhibited a peak at about (2θ) 14.15°, 18.8°, 19.89°, 24.26°, 26.84°, 31.12°, 36.74°, and 43.56° ( Figure S5-
A). As a result, it indicated that the green precursor has been converted successfully into the graphitic structure via the hydrothermal method [37]. The diffractogram of PLL-fGQDs exhibits peaks at about (2θ) 14.10°, 18.11°, 24.89°, 26.12°, 29.01°, 31.14°, 36°, and 41.80°. The use of PLL to functionalize GQDs manifested in an enhancement in the crystalline character of PLL-fGQDs ( Figure S5-
B). It might be because PLL tunes interlayer space and unstructured architecture. Similarly, the presence of oxygen and amine functionalities on the surface of PLL-fGQDs plays a central role. Figure S5-
C shows the diffractogram of fluorescent UiO-66@NH2 MOF exhibiting peaks at 8.81°, 25.12°, and 45.2° that assured the crystalline nature of UiO-66@NH2 MOF [38]. Fig. S5D represents the diffractogram of fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe exhibiting the peak at 9.8°, 25.89°, and 45.20°. Interestingly, the reduction in diffractogram peak intensity of UiO-66@NH2 MOF can be possible due to the incorporation of PLL-fGQDs in porous MOF [39].

In this work, the 'D' and 'G' band characteristics have provided crucial details regarding the effective production of the intended GQDs and PLL-fGQDs. In the GQDs, the 'D' bands at 1439 cm−1 (Fig. 3
-A) were obtained. It is because of ‘C’ atoms vibrating with dangling bonds in the disorganized ‘C’ framework. Besides, the 'G' band was formed at 1623 cm−1 due to the emergence of sp2 carbon atoms bearing vibrations of E2g phonon mode in the graphite cluster comprising a 2D hexagonal lattice. As well, the ID/IG ratio was 0.98 (less than one), indicating that the structure is comparable to graphite. Also, it validates the hydrophilic nature of GQDs [40]. Fig. 3 -B depicts the PLL-fGQDs displaying the presence of ‘D’ (1434 cm−1) and ‘G’ (1624 cm−1) bands. In this, ‘D’ bands get wider than the bare GQDs, which may be because of the functionalization of GQDs using PLL as a functionalizing agent. In addition, the ID/IG ratio was obtained at 0.92, which was less compared, to bare GQDs, which confirmed the higher disorder of arrangement in their graphitic structure. Fig. 3 -C portrays the Raman spectra of UiO-66@NH2 MOF displaying the existence of ‘D’ (1435 cm−1) and ‘G’ (1622 cm−1) bands with an ID/IG ratio of 1.08. In this spectrum, a peak at 1622 cm−1 assured the existence of a linker containing a C-C bond of an aromatic ring. Similarly, a wide peak at 1435 cm−1 confirmed the in-phase normal mode of carboxylate stretching [41]. Fig. 3 -D represents the Raman spectra of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe displaying the existence of ‘D’ (1430 cm−1) and ‘G’ (1614 cm−1) bands with ID/IG ratio (0.99) [42]. The presence of a combined peak of PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF with a slight alteration in their position and modification in intensity indicates the fabrication of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe.

Fig. S6-A depicts the SEM image of bare GQDs. Interestingly, the SEM image proved a spherical shape and smooth surface morphology of GQDs [43]. Figure S6-B depicts the SEM image of PLL-fGQDs wherein surface morphology is wrinkle-shaped, irregular surface area and sheet stack together to form a multi-layer structure. The morphology of PLL-fGQDs depicted the GQDs surface was converted irregularly shaped after surface modification, which might be because of PLL functionalization. Herein, surface energy is decreased by geometric wrinkling impacted by π- π interaction within sheets of GQDs. Figure S6-C depicts the SEM image of UiO-66@NH2 MOF in that surface morphology is porous, irregular, and a bit wrinkled on the surface. Consequently, it assured the production of UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Figure S6-D depicts PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe SEM images same as UiO-66@NH2 MOF wherein the surface morphology of the nanoprobe was obtained to be smooth whereas shape was found to be irregular. Further, HR-TEM research was undertaken to validate the embedding of PLL-fGQDs into porous UiO-66@NH2 MOF. The percent elemental composition of GQDs (Fig. 4
-A) was found to be 48.2 wt% of carbon (C), and 49.7 wt% of oxygen (O). As an output, it confirmed the conversion of black-eyed bean shells to a graphitic structure. Fig. 4-B depicts the EDAX spectra of PLL-fGQDs. The elemental composition of ‘N’, ‘C’, and ‘O’ were found to be 45.7 wt%, 55.12 wt%, and 31.35 wt% respectively. Herein, the existence of ‘N’ indicates occurrence of amino groups on the modulated surface of GQDs. As a result, the PLL-fGQDs with high nitrogen and hydroxyl functionalities were confirmed by the EDAX graph exhibiting a steep peak. After that, the EDAX spectra of UiO-66@NH2 MOF were depicted in Fig. 4-C. In this, zirconium (Zr), ‘N’, ‘C’, and ‘O’ were obtained to be 19.5 wt%, 5.8 wt%, 48.6 wt%, and 26.1 wt%, accordingly. Therefore, it assured the presence of UiO-66@NH2 MOF without any contaminating elements. The EDAX spectra of the proposed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe Fig. 4-D showed the presence of Zr, ‘N’, ‘C’, and ‘O’ with 11.5 wt%, 9.0 wt%, 45.9 wt%, and 33.6 wt%, accordingly authenticating the formation of a conjugate with pure form [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: HR-TEM, pore size and biocompatibility analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5-A depicts spherical and uniformly distributed PLL-fGQDs. The average size of PLL-fGQDs was found to be 3.90 ± 0.79 nm (N = 20) whereas the range of particle size was found to be 2.61 nm to 5.67 nm ( Figure S7-
A). Furthermore, it indicated that the PLL-fGQDs were distributed uniformly in water. Fig. 5 -B depicts that the obtained UiO-66@NH2 MOF synthesized has an octahedral structure and smooth surfaces. Furthermore, the average lateral dimension of prepared UiO-66@NH2 MOF was found to be 100 nm. The pore size of freeze-dried UiO-66NH2 MOF was found to be between 14 Å and 22 Å and 34 Å to 41 Å. It has an excellent pore size, which might be attributed to the freeze-drying of UiO-66@NH2 MOF ( Figure S7-
B). It may also aid in the incorporation of nanosized PLL-fGQDs (particle size less than 3.9 nm) into pores of UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Fig. 5 -C exhibits an HR-TEM image of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF, which analyzed the uniform distribution of green synthesized with functionalized PLL-fGQDs over the surface of UiO-66@NH2 MOF. In addition, it demonstrates typical particle size of the nanoprobe was found to be 118 nm. In conclusion, the HR-TEM image confirmed the successful formation of the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. We evaluated the biocompatibility of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF using the FHC CRL-1831 cell line (Fig. 5 -D). Specifically, we examined the impact of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe concentrations ranging from 25 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL on FHC CRL-1831 cells. Notably, at 150 μg/mL concentration, the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe demonstrated 89.23% cell viability. However, as the concentration of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe increased further from 175 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL, cell viability decreased from 81.20% to 73.55%, respectively. This observation confirms the effect of higher concentrations of nanoprobe on cell viability. Overall, the results affirm the favorable biocompatibility of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe with normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity analysis of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, several concentrations of synthesized UiO-66@NH2 MOF were explored to establish an appropriate concentration that could be employed with green synthesized PLL-fGQDs. The % QY of bare GQDs, and PLL-fGQDs was obtained to be 18.89 % and 41.33 %, respectively. The concentration of UiO-66@NH2 MOF reveals a direct linkage with the increment in fluorescence intensity. In this respect, a concentration of UiO-66@NH2 MOF of 160 µg/mL was considered suitable for the manufacturing of a PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe for taurine monitoring. Importantly, it has not displayed a proportionate connection with the inclusion of further levels of UiO-66@NH2 MOF, which might be ascribed to the perfect coupling of PLL-fGQDs with UiO-66-NH2 MOF. Afterward, PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF generated high fluorescence intensity as a synergistic output when compared to PLL-fGQDs and UiO-66@NH2 MOF termed as fluorescence “Switch-On”. Moreover, conjugating synthesized PLL-fGQDs increased the photosensitivity of UiO-66@NH2 MOF. Here, the stability study of fluorescence in PBS pH 7.4 provides 1.15 % of RSD (less than 5 %). Therefore, it confirmed the good stability of the proposed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe in pH 7.4 PBS for upto 7 days. Taurine sensing was investigated employing the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe with an optimal concentration-based response. Fig. 6
-A displays the optimization of Cu2+ ions concentration to reduce the fluorescence of PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. Here, the incorporation of Cu2+ ions from concentrations of 1 ng/mL to 260 ng/mL resulted in the quenching of fluorescence, a phenomenon known as fluorescence “Switch-Off”. Here, the concentration of Cu2+ ions from 1 ng/mL to 220 ng/mL shows the linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) with fluorescence quenching of nanoprobe (Fig. 6 -B). After optimization of the concentration of Cu2+ ions, the detection of taurine was performed. In this case, the initial addition of taurine lowest concentration (5 ng/mL) showed recovery of fluorescence. As well, the proposed quenched Cu2+-PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF system showed fluorescence recovery with a respective concentration range of taurine from 5 ng/mL to 380 ng/mL (Fig. 6 -C). In this, it showed the linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) between concentrations of taurine from 5 ng/mL to 360 ng/mL (Fig. 6 -D) and fluorescence recovery of quenched Cu2+-PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF system. Herein, Y = 12.03x + 922.6 were used for LOD and LOQ calculation. As a result, the LOD and LOQ were determined to be 2.91 ng/mL and 8.84 ng/mL, respectively. Assessing taurine with an extremely fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe indicated fluorescence recovery within the nanogram (ng) concentration range that confirmed high sensitivity. In this sensor, the addition of Cu2+ has a superior affinity for taurine-containing oxygen atoms of a sulfonic group and the nitrogen atom of an amine group which leads to the high specificity towards the taurine ( Fig. 7
-A). In addition, fluorescent UiO-66@NH2 MOF with PLL-fGQDs provide extremely high fluorescent intensity that leads to high sensitivity for the sensor whereas the incorporation of PLL-fGQDs can provide the support to avoid the collapse/modification of the framework of fluorescent MOF. Overall, the functionalization of GQDs using PLL and their incorporation into fluorescent MOF bestow high fluorescent properties. The comparison of numerous sensing techniques mentioned for a taurine estimate is provided in Table S1 .

The anti-interference performance of the fabricated fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe was verified by applying interfering agents. Fig. 7 -B depicts the blue fluorescence of the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe recovered by taurine concentrations. Numerous chosen interfering agents exhibited minimal capabilities to regain the luminous intensity of the synthesized PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe. Perhaps, it may be because of the high selectivity between taurine and Cu2+ ions. Interestingly, a blend of all interfering agents (sample Q) recovered the nanoprobe fluorescence slightly more than taurine. Ultimately, the PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe's anti-interference efficacy with taurine in the existence of varying biomolecules and ions was verified. As a result, the postulated PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe may be exploited for taurine biosensing in biological fluids. As well, the concentration of taurine (60 ng/mL and 40 ng/mL) was assessed in an artificial serum sample using a quenched Cu2+-PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe ( Table 1
). Consequently, it demonstrated taurine recovery rates of 96.26 % and 99.70 % in an artificial sample. The percent RSD was determined to be 2.07 % and 0.48 % (less than 5%), validating the real-time taurine assay employing a developed PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe [45]. The % RSD was found to be 0.92 % and 1.78 % for repeatability and stability, respectively. Here, the repeatability and stability of a proposed fluorescent PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe for the assessment of taurine in a sample confirmed the good stability and repeatability [46].

The present work provides the novel PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe-based fluorescence switch ‘On-Off-On’ approach for taurine monitoring. In brief, stable and nanosized GQDs were produced from green precursor via the hydrothermal method and then functionalized with PLL. It improved the optical and electrical properties of PLL-fGQDs more than plain GQDs. Moreover, stable, nanosized, and octahedral UiO-66@NH2 MOF were made employing the hydrothermal technique with 2-ATA as an organic linker. After that, green-prepared GQDs, PLL-fGQDs, UiO-66@NH2 MOF, and final customized PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe were characterized, according to spectroscopic characterizations to validate the synthesis. According to the sensing study, the Cu2+ ions in the fluorescent nanoprobe resulted in the dampening of a strong fluorescence. Besides, the addition of taurine into the prepared quenched nanoprobe offered the recovery of fluorescence in a directly proportional manner. Here, conjugation of PLL-fGQDs with UiO-66@NH2 MOF culminated in a considerable improvement in luminescence intensity that leads to the high sensitivity to nanoprobe. As a result, it provided a lower LOD than the currently reported methods. The coordination between taurine-containing functionality and Cu2+ ions provided a high selectivity to taurine. As well, spiked sample analysis, repeatability, and stability study assured the practical applicability of the sensor for the detection of taurine. To sum up, this nanosized design of fluorescence-based PLL-fGQDs@UiO-66@NH2 MOF nanoprobe can be utilized as a superior replacement for sensing taurine concentration in biological samples.
